sentences.text: When we came to Naliboki Pushcha, it was already endless woods, endless swamps.
Finally, somewhere, somehow, it got to the, to the Germans that there is a lot of partisans in the Naliboki Pushcha.
And they decided to eliminate ours.
So we had to leave the camp and run deeper into the woods.
We had to cross swamps and then find a little island again [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a drier spot.
And this is how we survived the attack.
They attacked us, I think, for about a week or so.
I remember it.
And it stands in my eyes like, like yesterday, how the-- their artillery, their, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, small missiles or the bullets of the artillery, they were flying over our head.
Or they will, they would fall ahead of us or behind us.
But some way, somehow, we had no casualties by pure luck.
And the Nazis were very mechanized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very clean dressed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would not want to get dirty, I guess, in the swamps.
Or they were somewhat afraid.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they would, would not get off the main road.
And this is the way we survived until the Russian army came in 1940-- middle of 1944--
Liberated us.
No, you had, you had horse and buggies.
If it's in the winter, you have, you have horses and sleds.
Some walked.
And whatever you had to take on the sled, you put on the sled, or on the buggy if it's in the summer.
No, this attack happened to be in the summer, was not winter.
All you do is carry your, carry your rifle, your armament.
That's it.
You leave everything else.
Nothing you can carry, just to be dressed and you armaments.
Well, there was, [SIGHS] unfortunately, one incident which one of my brothers killed one young fellow from our partisans.
He got shot for that.
They were divided by groups.
Let's say, these five or 10 young men with rifles, they take an X amount of women and children or older men, and then by groups.
Right, was assigned to every few young men.
And then one of my brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passed at the end.
If everybody went out of the camp.
And he saw a woman and two children crying.
He asked her, why would you-- why are you here?
Why aren't you with your group?
So she said that this, this group leader did not want to take her.
And she belonged to his group.
And this was the only Jewish man that my brother shot in order to keep order.
Was--
He got shot for that.
On the spot, in front of, in front of [? their truck, ?] 300, 400, at least 300, 400 young men with rifles.
They fight in front of the fighting group.
This was the only one during the entire course of the war that my brothers did that.
This was the discipline.
This was something he did, he did beyond any criticism, beyond any judgement, beyond-- it's an animal.
That's--
If he has to stay-- what do you call-- on watch, he has to stay two hours, he will stay six hours.
Or he was told that this is not, this is not a, a, a normal time.
And don't do this anymore.
This is unacceptable and this.
And you'll be surprised how the people were civilized.
There were never any rapes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There weren't any-- there were no rapes in our area.
In other words, in the Bielski brigade, there were no rapes.
If a woman wanted to be with him, to sleep with him, that's her business.
But if she turned him down, he wouldn't force her.
That was unacceptable.
And don't forget, this is wartime.
I mean, those are people with minds.
They know it's wartime.
If, if the rule is don't go force anybody to do anything, I mean, there was rules and regulations.
You better live by that.
Because a person's life means nothing.
There were, yes, the very tough ones, yes.
They did jokes.
They [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] would get drunk sometimes, you know, and--
As I said, it was needed very little, discipline.
All you had to be is told.
Whoever is-- by your commander, whoever is in charge.
If it's Pesach's group or if it's anybody else's group, he will tell him, look, this is unacceptable.
Don't do this no more.
Because if it will get to my brothers, you'll be in trouble.
And there is, there was very little punishment there.
Punishment is a bullet.
Very much so, a lot of drinking, yes.
Without the alcohol, I don't think they would be able to-- see, as depends how much alcohol a person takes in and how he can handle, how much alcohol he can handle.
Alcohol at times is very good, [BACKGROUND NOISE] very helpful.
A lot of drinking was, yes.
Yes, there was disagreements, as I told you before.
They went and wrote in a letter to the high Russian command that my brothers did wrong things, were doing wrong things.
When you get in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] six Jews together, this is my personal opinion I can give you, if you'll get in six Jews together, they will have seven parties.
Me, personally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I lived OK, because-- with some of the families.
I lived a lot with the Frydberg family, [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] used to eat a lot there, or other families.
I had no problem to-- I was sort of privileged somewhat.
So I had pretty good compared to others.
[SIGHS] Look, I grew up very quickly.
I don't think I had a normal childhood.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe a year into the war, and I was probably a man like anybody else.
I had, I had no normal childhood.
And I guess my best examples were my brothers.
Some of them are good, some of them were bad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I think most of them, I guess, at the end, they were good examples.
But they were good for the country over there, for that part of the world, not for America.
[LAUGHS] I wouldn't want to go into that.
It's too personal.
I wouldn't want to go into that.
I don't think so.
First of all, in a way, I was a very sad kid.
I was a very sad kid from the, from the time [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that I have seen they beat my father up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since then, my sadness remained up until this minute, in a way.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If I'll go into a restaurant, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't show this.
It's not their fault.
And if I won't act myself civilized, I'd be thrown out.
So I tried to be civilized like everybody else is.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] But to give you the best example really, is [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I understand life.
I accept as it comes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lived on borrowed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] time.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But I try to be as civilized as possible as other people around me are.
To share if you have some good, to help others, take care of my immediate family.
If it is possible to live nice together, try the best you can.
From October, '41, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till, I think, June or July-- it was warm-- 1944.
Russian army liberated us.
Oh, sure.
[SIGHS] Well, I can tell you what I remember through my-- as I felt.
I felt very sad, very miserable.
I guess I was the most miserable kid in those days, as far as I'm concerned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the war, you try.
You don't think about it because you trying to survive.
Now the war is finished, you can go anyplace you want.
You can go home.
But there is no home.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There is no home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They killed my parents.
And, and I remember the way my father was beaten up.
I think the most tragedy in my life was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that remained with me throughout my life, how they beat up my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This I never forget.
This I never forgot.
Oh, sure.
They butchered about 5,000 people through the weekend.
They start--
In Nowogrodek, Novogrudok.
[SIGHS] They digged two long holes like this.
In between those two holes, there was a little [? path ?] that only one person could stand and go through that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The holes were-- that they digged-- were about 100 meters, the length.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were three, four meters, the width.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they simply brought those people on this path between the two holes.
And that would-- they machine gunned them when you got on this path between those two digged, deep holes.
They machine gunned them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some fell in wounded, some dead.
This is the way they were butchered, over 5,000 Jews in one weekend.
And that's where I lost a few of my brothers and my parents.
In a suburb outside, in the woods.
Found out later, within a couple of weeks after that.
Of course.
We were in the woods at that time already.
If they arrested my parents, I had no one to be with at home.
In the ghetto, matter of a day or hours, maybe.
In the ghetto, in Novogrudok, they got them all together, the Jews from Novogrudok and the-- from the whole area, from the-- all the farmers who all lived in villages, they got them all together.
And that's when they butchered them.
Yes, most of them. 80% of the-- from our brigade were from ghetto of Novogrudok.
You see, they had already-- they had, they had-- I don't remember.
I think they had three sort of organized, in Jewish they would call it [NON-ENGLISH].
In other words, that they were butchered so many thousands in one shot.
Three times, they have done it.
The first time was in '41.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then was one in '42.
And I think the last one was in '43.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Most of the Jews that were in our brigade were from Novogrudok, 80%.
Yes.
Oh, sure.
Of course, I saw all the Nazis running.
And they were-- Russians-- the Russian soldiers arrived.
Of course, it was a very-- I don't know even how to describe it.
As a said, I can only tell you me, personally.
I was a very sad individual, very sad kid.
Because I knew you can go home.
And there is no home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this thought never left me, that there was no home.
That's it, forever.
And what it's going to be tomorrow, I don't know.
I went to Novogrudok.
I stood with some people there for a few months.
I don't think they're alive anymore.
[SIGHS] They're dead by now.
Stood with them.
I went to--
Yeah, some family and some not.
And then very shortly after that, my brothers came.
Who is together?
Of course, we lived together the forest.
But you asked me where was I in the city.
Oh, later on, they, they-- my brothers were not in Novogrudok.
They were in other places, in other cities.
I was the only one.
First of all-- 14.
And second, my brothers were married.
They had wives.
Strange people, strange people that knew me, that knew us.
That was no big deal, particularly right after the war.
It was a privilege for one Jew to help the other at that time if he could with anything.
As I said, I don't want to repeat this anymore, but I was a very sad kid at the time.
Then my brothers decided to quit Russia, to run away from Russia.
They came.
They took me.
And we run away from Russia.
We ran away to Western Europe.
No, no.
We were going frequently to our house out of the wood to see at night, you know, while during the course of the war.
After the war, I didn't want to go to the house, no.
What am I going to go over there?
Right after the war, no.
I did not want to see the house.
It was heartbreaking.
Did you ever see any of your neighbors?
40 years later, 35 years later, I went to see them, yes.
Wind up in Romania, from Romania to Yugoslavia, from Yugoslavia to Italy.
The first town in Italy, I think, was Trieste, if I remember correctly.
Then I was with a group of other kids.
And my brothers went to Israel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Palestine, in those days.
That was towards the end of '45.
Now we're in 1945.
I, I think those were Israeli people there that were sent from Palestine, from the Haganah, whatever.
And, and then, you could get across a lot of Jewish kids or teenagers like me all over Europe, Western or Eastern, that have no home, that had nobody.
Me, actually, I had brothers.
But you had a lot like me that had no one.
I was lucky that I had family in America.
I had brothers.
So I wasn't the worst off in the world yet.
From the Nazis?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh, god, from the Nazis, four brothers, my parents.
Abraham, Yankel, Zisl, Isak.
I think it was a small youth camp, which I was there very shortly.
I left on my own.
I left from Italy to France.
I just didn't want to stay there.
I didn't like it.
By myself, I went to Paris.
We're talking about the end of '45-- 15, 14,15.
Paris.
[LAUGHS] Where did I go in Paris?
[SIGHS] Where did--
